blurb: Cold-hardy, fire-blight-resistant pear, picked crisp and sweet in mid-summer.
spread: Clumping — needs a second pear (e.g. Luscious, Parker) to pollinate
seasons: White blossom spring; crisp green-red pears mid–late Aug
toxic: Fruit flesh edible; seeds contain cyanogenic amygdalin (mildly toxic if chewed in quantity)
origin: Europe to N Iraq (W Asia); cultivar bred at Univ. of Minnesota
habitat: Forest margins and scrubby hillsides across Europe and W Asia

care.hardiness: 'Summercrisp' is a University of Minnesota release (introduced 1985) bred for cold-climate growing, hardy to roughly USDA zone 3/4 — well within Boulder's 5b–6a range with margin to spare, so winter cold is not the limiting factor here.[1][2] It is also notably fire-blight-resistant, the trait that makes it a safe pear for the warm, moist springs of the Front Range that ravage susceptible cultivars.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant a dormant bare-root or container tree in spring (April–May on the Front Range), in full sun and well-drained soil; set the graft union above the soil line and water in well.[1][3] You need a second, compatible pear nearby for pollination (below), so plan space for two.[1] Pears are poor permanent container subjects this far inland: a potted tree's roots are much less cold-hardy than the top and can freeze in an exposed pot even in a mild zone-5 winter, so use a large (≥20 gal) container, sink or heel it in or shelter it (unheated garage) over winter, and plan to plant it in the ground before long.[3]
care.sun: Full sun — 6 or more hours of direct sun for the best crop and fruit quality.[1][3] Front-Range high-altitude sun is an asset; site the tree in the open, away from late-frost pockets.[3]
care.soil: Tolerates most well-drained soils across a near-neutral to slightly alkaline pH (about 6.0–8.0), so Boulder's clay-loam is fine if it drains.[3] Avoid waterlogged ground; amend heavy clay with organic matter and don't over-enrich — see Feeding.[1][3]
care.water: Moderate and regular: water newly planted trees about weekly through the first year or two, keeping the soil moist but never waterlogged; once established a deep soak during dry spells suffices.[1] Steady moisture during fruit fill improves size and quality.[1]
care.spacing: Standard pears are large — common pear can reach 25–40 ft — so give 'Summercrisp' room (roughly 15–20 ft between standard trees) and plant its pollinizer within that range so bees move between them.[1][3] On the Front Range it commonly matures around 18–25 ft tall.[1]
care.propagation: Like all fruit cultivars, 'Summercrisp' does not come true from seed and is propagated by budding or grafting the named scion onto a pear (or quince) rootstock — buy a grafted tree.[3] Seed-grown pears are random seedlings, not the cultivar, and pear seeds carry the cyanogenic compound noted under Edible parts.[3]
care.bloom: White spring blossom — but a single tree will not fruit well: 'Summercrisp', like most pears, needs a second, different pear variety blooming at the same time to cross-pollinate, such as 'Luscious', 'Parker', or 'Ure'.[1][2] Plant a compatible partner within bee range; bees do the work.[1][3]
care.feeding: Feed sparingly. Pears need little fertilizer once established, and excess nitrogen produces soft, succulent growth that is highly susceptible to fire blight — a real hazard on the Front Range — so go light and use a balanced feed in early spring only if growth is weak.[1] A mulched in-ground tree often needs no feeding at all.[4]
care.maintenance: Train and prune to a central leader (a single dominant trunk with tiered scaffold branches forming a pyramid), pruning every year in late winter/early spring after the coldest weather and before growth begins — not during bloom or for about two weeks after.[1][4] Remove water sprouts promptly and keep the canopy open. Fire-blight sanitation is part of routine care: cut out any blighted shoots (which look scorched/blackened) at least 6–8 in below the visibly affected wood, disinfecting tools between cuts, and remove the prunings.[1] 'Summercrisp's resistance reduces but does not eliminate the need for this.[1][2]
care.troubles: Fire blight (the bacterium *Erwinia amylovora*) is the headline pear disease, favored by warm, wet spring weather — 'Summercrisp' is resistant, which is its main selling point here, but practice sanitation and avoid over-fertilizing anyway.[1][2] Watch also for codling moth (larvae tunneling the fruit) and pear psylla (sap-feeding insects that excrete sticky honeydew), the standard pear insect pests, plus pear scab.[1][3] Keep the tree vigorous, the canopy open, and fallen fruit cleaned up.[1]
care.harvest: 'Summercrisp' is the unusual pear you pick — and eat — early. Harvest in mid-to-late August when the fruit is still firm and has turned from dark green to a lighter green with a red blush; unlike most pears it should NOT be allowed to ripen soft before eating — it's prized eaten crisp straight off the tree, and over-ripening turns it mealy.[1][2] (Most pears, by contrast, are picked mature-but-hard and ripened indoors; 'Summercrisp' is the exception.)[1] Pick promptly — wildlife takes any fruit left on the tree.[1]
care.companions: Plant its required pollinizer pear ('Luscious', 'Parker', or 'Ure') nearby, and keep turf and aggressive groundcovers back from the trunk to reduce competition and pest cover; underplant with low pollinator-friendly herbs (chives, alliums) rather than vigorous spreaders.[1][3] Mulch the root zone but keep the trunk flare clear.[4]

edible.summary: The pear fruit flesh is straightforwardly edible — 'Summercrisp' is grown to be eaten crisp and fresh; only the seeds carry a mild cyanogenic compound.[1][2]
edible.parts: The fruit flesh is the food. 'Summercrisp' is picked and eaten while still firm and crisp (not ripened soft like a dessert pear), sweet and good fresh out of hand; the flesh can also be cooked or canned.[1][2] The core and seeds are discarded.[3]
edible.preparation: Eat it fresh and crisp — that is the whole point of this cultivar — picking in mid-to-late August when firm and lightly blushed; do not let it ripen soft before eating, which turns it mealy.[1][2] Core it and discard the seeds.[3]
edible.caution: The fruit flesh is safe to eat. Like apple and other pome-fruit seeds, pear seeds contain a cyanogenic compound (amygdalin) that can release small amounts of hydrogen cyanide if the seeds are crushed and chewed — a hazard only if many seeds are deliberately chewed, not from eating the fruit normally.[3] Discard the core and seeds and don't chew them; the flesh itself poses no such risk.[3]
